content_markdown: A fixed mass of an ideal gas undergoes an isochoric (isovolumetric) change. This increases the pressure of the gas. Which describes the change of internal energy of the gas and the direction of transfer of thermal energy?

diagram_metadata.description: A table showing four options (A-D) with columns for 'Internal energy' and 'Direction of transfer of thermal energy'.
labels_to_preserve: Direction of transfer of thermal energy

options.label: A
options.text: Internal energy: increase; Direction of transfer of thermal energy: to the gas

options.label: B
options.text: Internal energy: increase; Direction of transfer of thermal energy: from the gas

options.label: C
options.text: Internal energy: decrease; Direction of transfer of thermal energy: to the gas

options.label: D
options.text: Internal energy: decrease; Direction of transfer of thermal energy: from the gas

vibe_explanation: In an isochoric process, the volume is constant, so no work is done ($W = 0$). According to the ideal gas law ($PV = nRT$), if pressure increases at constant volume, the temperature must increase. Since internal energy of an ideal gas is proportional to temperature, internal energy increases. By the first law of thermodynamics ($Q = \Delta U + W$), if $\Delta U$ is positive and $W$ is zero, $Q$ must be positive, meaning thermal energy is transferred to the gas.
